datasets.title: Adult Social Care
datasets.summary: Requests for support, long-term care recipients, spending trends, workforce vacancies and turnover, care worker pay, CQC provider ratings, unpaid carers, and self-funders.

requestsForSupport.title: Requests for Support
requestsForSupport.summary: 2.02 million new requests for adult social care support in 2024-25, up from 1.80 million in 2015-16.

longTermSupport.title: Long-Term Support Recipients
longTermSupport.summary: 672,000 people receiving long-term support as at March 2025. Working-age adults rising, over-65s fell then recovered.

spending.title: Adult Social Care Spending
spending.summary: Net expenditure rose from £17.1bn in 2010-11 to £27.6bn in 2024-25. Declined in real terms 2010-15, then recovered.

workforce.title: Workforce
workforce.summary: 1.60 million filled posts in 2024-25. Vacancy rate fell to 5.9% from 10.6% peak in 2021-22. Turnover 24%.

pay.title: Care Worker Pay
pay.summary: Median care worker pay £12.60/hr in December 2025, just 39p above the National Living Wage of £12.21.

cqcRatings.title: CQC Ratings
cqcRatings.summary: 82% of adult social care providers rated Good or Outstanding in 2024. 16% Requires Improvement, 1% Inadequate.

unpaidCarers.title: Unpaid Carers
unpaidCarers.summary: 4.7 million unpaid carers in England (Census 2021). 1.4 million provide 50+ hours per week.

selfFunders.title: Self-funders
selfFunders.summary: 37% of care home residents self-fund, 26% of community care users. Self-funders pay ~40% more than publicly funded clients.

datasets.title: Airports
datasets.summary: UK airport passenger traffic, air transport movements, capacity trends, and airport rankings. 292 million passengers across 49 airports in 2024, with ~5,800 commercial flights per day. Flights peaked at 2.35M in 2007 and have not recovered to that level. COVID collapsed traffic to 74 million passengers in 2020.

annualPassengers.title: Total Terminal Passengers
annualPassengers.summary: UK airport passengers grew from 228M (2005) to 297M (2019), collapsed to 74M (2020), recovered to 292M (2024).

annualMovements.title: Air Transport Movements
annualMovements.summary: Flights peaked at 2.35M in 2007, declined post-financial crisis, plateaued at 2.2M (2017-19), collapsed to 812K (2020), recovered to 2.01M (2024) — still 86% of 2007 peak. No major new runway capacity added.

paxPerFlight.title: Passengers per Flight
paxPerFlight.summary: Average passengers per air transport movement rose from 101 (2005) to 145 (2024), reflecting larger aircraft and higher load factors. COVID distorted the metric (2020-21).

passengersByAirport.title: Passengers by Airport (2024)
passengersByAirport.summary: Heathrow leads with 84M passengers (29% of UK total). Gatwick 43M, Manchester 31M, Stansted 30M. 49 airports reported traffic.

movementsByAirport.title: Air Transport Movements by Airport (2024)
movementsByAirport.summary: Total 2.1M air transport movements in 2024. Heathrow 474k, Gatwick 262k, Stansted 188k.

airportLocations.title: UK Airport Locations with Traffic Data (2024)
airportLocations.summary: Geographic coordinates, passenger counts, and movement figures for 47 UK airports. Used for map visualization. Heathrow (51.47N, 0.45W) leads with 84M passengers.

datasets.title: Asylum & Irregular Migration Statistics
datasets.summary: Comprehensive asylum system data: Channel crossings (299 in 2018 to 36,816 in 2024), asylum applications and decisions (back to 2001), backlog (peaked at 170k in Q1 2023, now 97k), grant rates by nationality and over time, returns and removals (halved since 2013), appeals (49% overturn rate), accommodation costs (£4.7bn peak), international per-capita comparison, processing times, detention, resettlement schemes, demographics, modern slavery referrals, and caseworker staffing.

channelCrossings.title: Channel Crossings (Small Boats)
channelCrossings.summary: Annual small boat crossings of the English Channel, 2018-2024. Rose from 299 in 2018 to peak of 45,774 in 2022, then 36,816 in 2024.

channelCrossingsQuarterly.title: Channel Crossings Quarterly
channelCrossingsQuarterly.summary: Quarterly small boat crossings showing seasonal pattern — crossings peak in Q3 (summer). Range from 476 (Q1 2020) to 18,754 (Q3 2022).

asylumDecisions.title: Asylum Applications and Initial Decisions
asylumDecisions.summary: Applications, decisions, grants and refusals 2001-2024. Applications peaked at 84k in 2002 and 75k in 2022. Grant rate rose from 8% (2004) to 93% (2021), now 58% (2024).

grantRateByNationality.title: Asylum Grant Rate by Nationality (2023)
grantRateByNationality.summary: Grant rates vary dramatically by nationality: Afghanistan 98%, Syria 98%, Albania 53%, India 18%. Based on initial decisions year ending December 2023.

grantRateTimeSeries.title: Grant Rates by Nationality Over Time
grantRateTimeSeries.summary: How grant rates shifted 2019-2024 for key nationalities. Albania dropped from 47% to 21%. Afghanistan remained above 95%. Iran fell from 62% to 52%.

applicationsByNationality.title: Asylum Applications by Nationality
applicationsByNationality.summary: Top nationalities applying for asylum 2018-2024. Albanian applications surged to 16,052 in 2022, fell to 2,147 by 2024. Afghan applications rose steadily.

asylumBacklog.title: Asylum Cases Awaiting Initial Decision
asylumBacklog.summary: Backlog grew from 18,195 (2010) to peak 160,919 (2022), now reducing to 96,577 (2024). The surge reflects applications outpacing decisions.

asylumBacklogQuarterly.title: Asylum Backlog Quarterly
asylumBacklogQuarterly.summary: Quarterly backlog 2019-2024. Peaked at 170,865 in Q1 2023. Steady reduction since as decision-making ramped up.

asylumAppeals.title: Asylum Appeals (First-tier Tribunal)
asylumAppeals.summary: Appeals lodged, determined, and outcomes 2014-2024. Overturn rate rose from 29% (2014) to 63% (2022), now 49% (2024). Many initial refusals are reversed.

dublinTransfers.title: Dublin III Transfers
dublinTransfers.summary: UK Dublin transfers 2015-2020 (ceased with Brexit). UK was consistently a net recipient — more transfers in than out every year.

returns.title: Returns and Removals
returns.summary: Total returns halved from 41,017 (2013) to 20,040 (2024). Enforced removals fell from 18,280 (2006) to 7,102 (2024). FNO removals around 4,000/year.

internationalComparison.title: Asylum Applications per 100,000 Population
internationalComparison.summary: Per-capita asylum applications 2015-2023 for UK, Germany, France, Sweden, Italy, Austria, Netherlands, Spain. UK consistently among lowest in Europe.

accommodationCosts.title: Asylum Accommodation Costs
accommodationCosts.summary: Annual accommodation costs rose from £540m (2015-16) to £4.7bn (2023-24) peak. Hotel use peaked at 51,000 in 2022-23, now declining.

processingTimes.title: Asylum Processing Times
processingTimes.summary: Median weeks to initial decision, 2014-2024. Rose from 14 weeks (2015) to 60 weeks (2022), now reduced to 32 weeks (2024).

processingComparison.title: Processing Times International (2023)
processingComparison.summary: UK median 52 weeks vs Sweden 14, Netherlands 17, France 21, Germany 28 in 2023.

irregularRoutes.title: Irregular Migration by Route
irregularRoutes.summary: Visa overstayers (49-62k) exceed Channel crossings (2-46k) every year. Lorry detections declining (6.1k to 2.4k).

detention.title: Immigration Detention
detention.summary: Persons entering detention fell from 32k (2015) to 23k (2024). Detention population snapshot from 3,030 (2015) to 1,842 (2024).

resettlement.title: Resettlement Schemes (Safe/Legal Routes)
resettlement.summary: VPRS peaked at 5,241 (2019). Afghan schemes (ARAP+ACRS) brought 6,532 in 2021. Total around 5,500/year. Does not include Homes for Ukraine.

crossingsDemographics.title: Channel Crossings Demographics
crossingsDemographics.summary: Adult men share fell from 87% (2020) to 74% (2024). Children rose from 8% to 16%. Women from 5% to 10%.

modernSlaveryReferrals.title: Modern Slavery NRM Referrals
modernSlaveryReferrals.summary: National Referral Mechanism referrals rose from 3,805 (2016) to 17,004 (2023). Many referrals come from individuals in the asylum system.

uasc.title: Unaccompanied Asylum-Seeking Children
uasc.summary: UASC applications from 1,945 (2014) to 5,242 peak (2022), now 3,817 (2024). Local authorities bear accommodation costs.

caseworkerStaffing.title: Asylum Caseworker Staffing
caseworkerStaffing.summary: FTE decision-makers surged from 340 (2020) to 2,150 (2024). This sixfold increase is driving faster backlog reduction.

globalDisplacement.title: Global Forced Displacement
globalDisplacement.summary: Total forcibly displaced people worldwide rose from 40m (2001) to 123m (2024), nearly tripling. Driven by Syria (13.8m), Ukraine (11.7m), Sudan (10.8m), Afghanistan (6.4m). This global surge is the structural context behind rising asylum applications across all European countries.

conflictDisplacement.title: Major Conflicts Driving Displacement
conflictDisplacement.summary: Ten largest displacement crises: Syria (13.8m), Ukraine (11.7m), Sudan (10.8m), DR Congo (7.3m), Afghanistan (6.4m), Iraq (4.6m), Yemen (4.5m), South Sudan (4.4m), Myanmar (1.3m), Eritrea (0.6m).

conflictVsNonConflict.title: UK Asylum: Conflict vs Non-Conflict Origin
conflictVsNonConflict.summary: Applicants from conflict-origin countries (>70% grant rate) rose from 45% of applications in 2004 to 52-64% in recent years. This compositional shift towards genuine refugees is the primary driver of rising overall grant rates.

datasets.title: Borrowing & Debt
datasets.summary: UK government borrowing, gilt yields, debt interest, public sector net debt, gilt maturity profile, issuance remit, international debt comparison, and sovereign credit ratings.

giltYields.title: Gilt Yields by Maturity
giltYields.summary: Monthly average benchmark gilt yields at 2, 5, 10 and 30-year maturities. 10-year yield rose from 1% in Jan 2022 to 4.89% in Mar 2026.

monthlyBorrowing.title: Monthly Public Sector Net Borrowing
monthlyBorrowing.summary: Monthly borrowing by financial year. Year to Feb 2026: £125.9bn. Full year 2024-25: £151.8bn.

annualBorrowing.title: Annual Borrowing Trend
annualBorrowing.summary: Public sector net borrowing in £bn and % of GDP from 1993-94 to 2029-30 (forecast). Peak: 14.7% GDP in 2020-21.

debtInterest.title: Debt Interest Payments
debtInterest.summary: Central government debt interest payments reached post-war high of £112.1bn (4.3% GDP) in 2022-23 due to index-linked gilt inflation.

debtToGdp.title: Public Sector Net Debt as % of GDP
debtToGdp.summary: PSND rose from 30% of GDP in 2001-02 to 95.6% in 2024-25. Stabilised in mid-90s range.

maturityProfile.title: Gilt Maturity Profile
maturityProfile.summary: Outstanding gilt portfolio by maturity bucket. Average maturity approximately 14.8 years. 25% index-linked.

giltIssuance.title: Gilt Issuance Remit
giltIssuance.summary: Gross gilt issuance £296.9bn in 2024-25. 2025-26 remit: £299.6bn gross, £164.7bn redemptions.

intlDebtGdp.title: International Debt Comparison
intlDebtGdp.summary: General government gross debt as % of GDP. UK at 101.3%, Japan 252.7%, US 123.8%, Germany 62.9%.

creditRatings.title: UK Sovereign Credit Ratings
creditRatings.summary: S&P: AA (stable), Moody's: Aa3 (stable), Fitch: AA- (stable).

datasets.title: Buses
datasets.summary: Bus passenger journeys, fares, and fleet size. GB total journeys fell from 4.9bn (2006/07) to 4.1bn (2024/25). London accounts for 45% of all journeys. Bus fares have risen 108% since 2005, compared to 77% for CPI. The GB bus fleet fell from 52,190 to 35,065 vehicles over the same period.

journeysByRegion.title: Passenger Journeys by Region
journeysByRegion.summary: Annual bus journeys by area from 2004/05. London grew from 1.8bn to 1.8bn (stable). English metro areas fell from 1.0bn to 0.8bn. Scotland fell from 459M to 335M. Wales fell from 123M to 72M.

journeysHistorical.title: Bus Patronage Since 1950
journeysHistorical.summary: Long-run GB bus journeys from 1949/50. Peaked at 13.2bn in 1954/55, declined to 4.1bn by 2024/25. Car ownership drove the decline from the 1960s.

faresIndex.title: Bus Fares Index
faresIndex.summary: Quarterly fares index (March 2005 = 100). GB fares index reached 208 by Q4 2025. England outside London rose to 214, London to 196. CPI comparison included at 177.

fleetSize.title: Fleet Size by Area
fleetSize.summary: Number of registered buses by area. GB fleet fell from 52,190 (2004/05) to 35,065 (2024/25). London fleet stable at ~9,000. Largest decline in English non-metro areas.

concessionaryPct.title: Concessionary Journeys as % of Total
concessionaryPct.summary: Share of bus journeys made using free concessionary passes. England 28%, Scotland 55% (after under-22s scheme in 2022), Wales 45%. England's share fell from 32% pre-pandemic.

passHolders.title: Concessionary Pass Holders (England)
passHolders.summary: 8.9 million concessionary passes in England (2024/25). 89% older persons, 11% disabled. Average 70 journeys per pass, down from 110 in 2010/11.

expenditure.title: Concessionary Travel Expenditure
expenditure.summary: Net local authority expenditure on concessionary travel in England: £1.02bn in 2024/25. Operator reimbursement £795m. Average reimbursement £1.27 per journey.

datasets.title: Children's Social Care
datasets.summary: Children looked after numbers and rates, foster carer supply, social worker caseloads and vacancies, referrals and child protection plans, spending, care leaver outcomes, and placement types.

childrenLookedAfter.title: Children Looked After
childrenLookedAfter.summary: 83,630 children in care in England in 2024, rate of 70 per 10,000. Risen 30% since 2010.

fosterCarers.title: Approved Foster Carers
fosterCarers.summary: 56,345 approved mainstream foster carers as at March 2025, down 12% from 63,890 in 2021.

fosterRecruitment.title: Foster Carer Recruitment vs Deregistration
fosterRecruitment.summary: 4,430 new foster households approved in 2024-25, 4,690 deregistered. Net loss of 260 households.

socialWorkerWorkforce.title: Social Worker Workforce
socialWorkerWorkforce.summary: Average caseload 15.4 per worker in 2024, vacancy rate 18.0%, agency rate 15.8%.

referralsAndProtection.title: Referrals & Child Protection
referralsAndProtection.summary: 621,880 referrals in 2023-24, 224,990 Section 47 enquiries, 49,960 children on protection plans.

spending.title: Children's Social Care Spending
spending.summary: Total spending rose from £7.4bn in 2010-11 to £14.2bn in 2024-25. Per child in care: £72,000.

careLeavers.title: Care Leaver Outcomes
careLeavers.summary: 54% of care leavers aged 19-21 in EET in 2024, vs ~85% of all young people. 89% in suitable accommodation.

placementTypes.title: Placement Types
placementTypes.summary: 67% in foster care, 10% in children's homes, 7% in supported accommodation, 6% placed with parents.

datasets.title: Consumer Prices (CPIH)
datasets.summary: Consumer Prices Index including owner-occupier housing costs, broken down by category.

datasets.title: Culture & Religion
datasets.summary: Religious affiliation from the Census (2001, 2011, 2021). Christianity declined from 71.7% to 46.2%, while 'no religion' rose from 14.8% to 37.2%. No census has been conducted since March 2021.

religionPct.title: Religious Affiliation (% of population)
religionPct.summary: Share of population by religion across three census years. Christian fell from 71.7% (2001) to 46.2% (2021). No religion rose from 14.8% to 37.2%.

religionAbsolute.title: Religious Affiliation (millions)
religionAbsolute.summary: Population by religion in millions. Christians fell from 37.3M (2001) to 27.5M (2021). Those reporting no religion rose from 7.7M to 22.2M.

datasets.title: Defence & Security
datasets.summary: Defence spending, armed forces personnel, recruitment and retention, equipment inventory, fleet trends, international capability comparison, procurement affordability, major programme delivery, industry concentration, and competition rates.

spendingPctGdp.title: Defence Spending as % of GDP
spendingPctGdp.summary: UK defence spending fell from 3.8% GDP in 1990 to 2.3% in 2024, above NATO's 2% target but below the new 2.5% aspiration.

spendingReal.title: Defence Spending in Real Terms
spendingReal.summary: Real defence spending fell from £46bn in 2009 to £38bn in 2016, recovering to £54.2bn in 2024 (2023-24 prices).

intlComparison.title: NATO Spending Comparison
intlComparison.summary: Defence spending as % GDP across NATO allies. Poland leads (3.9%), UK at 2.3%, several allies below 2%.

equipmentPlan.title: Equipment Plan (10-Year)
equipmentPlan.summary: MoD 10-year equipment plan: £60bn total. Submarines & nuclear £17.2bn, combat air £12.5bn, ships £8.3bn.

personnel.title: Armed Forces Personnel
personnel.summary: UK regular forces declined 39% from 207.7k in 2000 to 125.7k in 2025. Army from 110k to 70k.

reserves.title: Reserve Forces Strength
reserves.summary: Trained reserve strength peaked at 30.7k in 2022, declined to 26k in 2024, recovered to 29.1k in 2025.

strengthVsRequirement.title: Trained Strength vs Requirement
strengthVsRequirement.summary: All services below target: Army -3%, RN/RM -8%, RAF -13%. Overall 6% (8,590 personnel) below requirement.

intakeOutflow.title: Recruitment Intake vs Outflow
intakeOutflow.summary: 13,450 joined in 2024-25 (up 19%), 14,600 left (down 7%). Outflow has exceeded intake in most years since 2000.

voluntaryOutflow.title: Voluntary Outflow Rate
voluntaryOutflow.summary: Voluntary outflow rate peaked at 6.4% in 2023-24, fell to 5.9% in 2024-25. Army highest at 6.3%.

currentEquipment.title: Current Equipment Inventory
currentEquipment.summary: Major platforms at 1 April 2025: 288 Challenger 2 MBT, 129 Typhoon, 41 F-35B, 50 Apache, 6 Type 45 destroyers, 7 Type 23 frigates, 10 submarines.

escortFleet.title: Escort Fleet Trend
escortFleet.summary: Royal Navy escort fleet contracted from 48 ships (1990) to 13 (2025). Type 26/31 programmes will restore frigates to 13.

combatAircraft.title: Combat Aircraft Fleet Trend
combatAircraft.summary: Combat-capable fast jets declined from 250 (2000) to 170 (2025). Tornado and Harrier retired; Typhoon and F-35B now form fleet.

intlEquipment.title: International Equipment Comparison
intlEquipment.summary: UK equipment vs peer nations (France, Germany, Italy, Japan, S. Korea, Turkey, Australia). UK has fewer tanks than France/Germany but unique carrier and nuclear sub capability.

equipmentPlanAffordability.title: Equipment Plan Affordability
equipmentPlanAffordability.summary: NAO annual assessment of MoD Equipment Plan. Funding gap rose from zero in 2012-22 to £16.9bn in 2023-33, the largest since reporting began.

majorProgrammes.title: Major Programme Cost & Schedule
majorProgrammes.summary: 8 major programmes tracked: Ajax (77% cost growth, paused), Type 26 (114% growth), Dreadnought (11% growth), F-35B (21% growth), Boxer (79% growth). Most delayed from original in-service dates.

industryConcentration.title: Industry Concentration
industryConcentration.summary: BAE Systems received 16.3% of MoD procurement spend (£6.7bn) in 2024-25, the highest single-supplier share since records began. Top 10 suppliers account for 39%.

competitionRate.title: Competition vs Non-Competitive Procurement
competitionRate.summary: Competitive procurement fell from 31% of MoD spend in 2014-15 to 8% in 2024-25. Non-competitive sourcing rose to 45%, driven by nuclear and combat air programmes.

smeShare.title: SME Share of MoD Procurement
smeShare.summary: Direct SME share of MoD expenditure remained around 3.5-5% since 2016-17. Including indirect spending through prime contractors, total SME share was 25.5% in 2021-22.

datasets.title: Education & Skills
datasets.summary: Per-pupil spending, GCSE results, PISA scores, HE participation, teacher workforce, and degree classification trends.

perPupilSpending.title: Per-Pupil Spending (real terms)
perPupilSpending.summary: Real per-pupil spending peaked at £6,720 in 2009-10, fell 6% to £6,310 by 2023-24.

gcseResults.title: GCSE Grade 5+ in English & Maths
gcseResults.summary: Share of students achieving grade 5+ in English and Maths. Grading changed from A*-G to 9-1 in 2017.

pisaScores.title: PISA International Scores
pisaScores.summary: UK PISA scores in maths (489), reading (494), and science (503) as of 2022.

teacherWorkforce.title: Teacher Workforce
teacherWorkforce.summary: Number of teachers and pupil-teacher ratios in state schools.

datasets.title: Environment & Air Quality
datasets.summary: Greenhouse gas emissions, PM2.5 and NO2 air quality, and ultra-low emission vehicle uptake.

datasets.title: Ethnicity & Demographics
datasets.summary: Census ethnicity data across 2001, 2011, and 2021. White British fell from 87.5% (2001) to 74.4% (2021). Asian population grew from 4.4% to 9.3%. Black population grew from 2.2% to 4.0%. Mixed grew from 1.3% to 2.9%. Total population rose from 52.0M to 59.6M.

byCensus.title: Broad Ethnic Groups by Census Year
byCensus.summary: Population share by broad ethnic group (White, Asian, Black, Mixed, Other) across 2001, 2011, and 2021 censuses. White fell from 91.3% to 81.7%, Asian rose from 4.4% to 9.3%.

detailed2021.title: Detailed Ethnic Groups (2021 Census)
detailed2021.summary: All 16 ethnic group categories from Census 2021 with counts and percentages. Largest groups: White British (44.4M, 74.4%), White Other (3.7M, 6.2%), Asian Indian (1.9M, 3.1%).

timeRange.start: White: English, Welsh, Scottish, Northern Irish, British

datasets.title: Sterling Exchange Rates
datasets.summary: Monthly average GBP/USD and GBP/EUR exchange rates and the Sterling Effective Exchange Rate Index (trade-weighted). GBP/USD fell from 1.62 in Jan 2000 to around 1.29 in 2026. The pound fell sharply after the 2016 EU referendum and during the 2022 mini-budget.

monthly.title: Sterling Exchange Rates (Monthly Average)
monthly.summary: GBP/USD, GBP/EUR spot rates and Sterling ERI from Jan 2000. GBP/USD fell from 1.62 (2000) to 1.29 (2026). GBP/EUR fell from 1.61 (2000) to 1.19 (2026). Sterling ERI declined from 107 to 83.

datasets.title: Family, Fertility & Households
datasets.summary: Birth rates, fertility by parity, mean age of motherhood, family structure, and household composition. Covers 1960 to present.

tfrByOrder.title: Total Fertility Rate by Birth Order
tfrByOrder.summary: UK TFR fell from 2.72 in 1960 to 1.41 in 2024, now 33% below replacement. Rising childlessness is the primary driver.

meanAgeByOrder.title: Mean Age of Mother by Birth Order
meanAgeByOrder.summary: Mean age at first birth rose from 24.4 in 1960 to 29.5 in 2022, a shift of over 5 years.

birthsSeries.title: Live Births
birthsSeries.summary: Annual live births in England & Wales. Peaked at 876k in 1964, now around 595k.

familyTypeSeries.title: Family Type
familyTypeSeries.summary: Breakdown of families by type: married couples, cohabiting couples, and lone parents.

householdSizeSeries.title: Average Household Size
householdSizeSeries.summary: Average number of people per household. Fell from 3.09 in 1961 to 2.35 in 2024.

datasets.title: Gross Domestic Product (GDP)
datasets.summary: Quarterly GDP in real terms (chained volume measures), GDP per capita, and quarter-on-quarter growth. Real GDP reached £706bn in 2025 Q4. GDP per capita was £10,127. Quarterly data from 1990 to present.

quarterly.title: UK GDP (Quarterly)
quarterly.summary: Real GDP (chained volume, £bn), GDP per capita (£), and quarter-on-quarter growth (%). GDP grew from £355bn in 1990-Q1 to £706bn in 2025-Q4. Contracted during 1991, 2008-09, and 2020 recessions.

datasets.title: General Practice
datasets.summary: GP appointments, patient satisfaction, same-day access, practice consolidation, workforce (headcount vs FTE), and training pipeline.

appointments.title: GP Appointments by Type
appointments.summary: Around 30m GP appointments per month. Online grew from 4% to ~20% since COVID.

appointmentWait.title: Time from Booking to Appointment
appointmentWait.summary: ~45% of GP appointments are same-day, 73% within a week. Stable since 2019.

satisfaction.title: Patient Satisfaction (GPPS)
satisfaction.summary: Overall good fell from 85% (2018) to 71% (2023). Phone access hardest-hit.

practices.title: Practice Count & List Size
practices.summary: ~2,000 practices closed/merged since 2013. Avg list size rose from 6,900 to 9,900.

trainingPipeline.title: GP Training Pipeline
trainingPipeline.summary: ST1 posts expanded to 4,276 (100% filled). 10,900 trainees in system but conversion lossy.

workforce.title: GP Workforce (Headcount vs FTE)
workforce.summary: Headcount grew to 35,900 but FTE fell to 27,544 — driven by part-time working, not fewer GPs.

datasets.title: Hate Crime
datasets.summary: Police-recorded hate crimes by monitored strand from 2011/12 to 2024/25 (excl. Metropolitan Police for comparability). 115,990 total offences in 2024/25. Race is the largest strand (82,490), followed by sexual orientation (18,702), disability (10,224), religion (7,164), and transgender (3,809). Recording improvements since 2014 mean increases partly reflect better police practice, not necessarily higher prevalence. Religious hate crimes are broken down by perceived religion: Muslim (3,199), Jewish (1,715), Christian (401), Sikh (202), Hindu (118).

byStrand.title: Hate Crimes by Monitored Strand
byStrand.summary: Police-recorded hate crimes by strand (race, religion, sexual orientation, disability, transgender) from 2011/12 to 2024/25. Excludes Metropolitan Police. Race accounts for 71% of all hate crime motivating factors. Total rose from 33,950 in 2011/12 to 122,389 in 2024/25, partly due to improved recording.

byReligion.title: Religious Hate Crimes by Perceived Religion
byReligion.summary: Religious hate crimes by perceived religion of the victim (2021/22-2024/25, excl. Met Police). Muslim victims account for 46% of religious hate crimes (3,199), Jewish victims 24% (1,715). Jewish hate crimes peaked at 2,093 in 2023/24 following events in the Middle East.

datasets.title: Health Outcomes
datasets.summary: Life expectancy at birth by sex, cancer survival rates by type, and avoidable mortality trends.

lifeExpectancy.title: Life Expectancy at Birth
lifeExpectancy.summary: UK life expectancy reached 79.0 (male) and 82.9 (female) in 2023, still below 2014 peak after COVID setback.

cancerSurvival.title: Cancer Survival Rates
cancerSurvival.summary: 1-year and 5-year net survival by cancer site. Ranges from 7% (pancreas) to 92% (melanoma) at 5 years.

avoidableMortality.title: Avoidable Mortality
avoidableMortality.summary: Age-standardised avoidable mortality rate fell from 257 per 100k in 2001 to 201 in 2019, spiked during COVID.

datasets.title: Hospital Capacity
datasets.summary: NHS overnight bed numbers by type, bed occupancy rates, international comparison (OECD), and hospital admissions by type.

beds.title: Overnight Beds by Type
beds.summary: Total NHS overnight beds fell from 186k in 2000-01 to 101k in 2025-26, a 46% reduction.

occupancy.title: Bed Occupancy Rate
occupancy.summary: General & acute bed occupancy rose from 82% in 2000-01 to 90.5% in 2025-26.

international.title: International Comparison (OECD)
international.summary: UK has 2.4 beds per 1,000 population vs OECD average of 4.3.

admissions.title: Hospital Admissions
admissions.summary: 20.6m admissions in 2024-25: 8.6m day cases, 6.6m emergency, 5.4m elective.

datasets.title: Hospital & Construction Infrastructure
datasets.summary: Construction output by sector (1997-2025) and health construction new orders (1985-2025). 'Public other new work' (which includes hospitals and schools) was £14.3bn in 2025, down from a peak of £22bn in 2010 during the Building Schools for the Future and hospital building programmes. Health construction new orders totalled £2.4bn in 2025. Private commercial construction (£24bn) dwarfs public non-housing (£14bn). Infrastructure spending (£35bn) has grown substantially since 2010.

constructionOutput.title: Construction Output by Sector
constructionOutput.summary: Annual construction output in £m (chained volume, SA). Public other new work peaked at £22bn in 2010 and fell to £12bn by 2016, recovering to £14bn in 2025. Infrastructure rose from £17bn in 1997 to £35bn in 2025. Private commercial peaked at £32bn in 2017.

healthOrders.title: Health Construction New Orders
healthOrders.summary: Annual value of new orders for public health construction from 1985. Peaked at £4.7bn in 2007 during Labour's hospital building programme, fell to £1.0bn in 2013, recovered to £2.4bn in 2025. Current prices.

datasets.title: Housing Density & Geography
datasets.summary: Population density by UK city, international city density comparison, and average house prices by region.

ukCityDensity.title: UK City Population Density
ukCityDensity.summary: London at 5,598/km², other cities 1,500-4,700/km², England average 434/km².

regionalPrices.title: Average House Price by Region
regionalPrices.summary: London £523k, South East £375k, North East £160k. Approximately 3:1 ratio London to cheapest region.

datasets.title: Housing Stock
datasets.summary: Housing stock by period built, dwelling type breakdown, tenure split over time (owner-occupied, private rented, social rented), and EPC energy ratings by dwelling age.

stockByAge.title: Housing Stock by Period Built
stockByAge.summary: 20% of English homes built before 1919. Post-war (1945-1980) makes up 37%.

stockByType.title: Dwelling Type Breakdown
stockByType.summary: Semi-detached (26%) and terraced (25%) dominate. Purpose-built flats at 17%.

tenureTrend.title: Tenure Split Over Time
tenureTrend.summary: Owner-occupation peaked at 71% in early 2000s, now 65%. Private renting rose from 10% (1990) to 19%.

epcByAge.title: EPC Rating by Dwelling Age
epcByAge.summary: Only 2% of pre-1919 homes rated EPC A-B vs 55% of post-2002 homes. 45% of pre-1919 rated E or below.

datasets.title: Housebuilding
datasets.summary: Net additional dwellings, new build completions by tenure (private, housing association, local authority), average new build floor area, international size comparison, EPC new build lodgements as a leading indicator, and brick delivery volumes.

netAdditions.title: Net Additional Dwellings
netAdditions.summary: Total annual increase in housing stock. 208,600 in 2024-25, below the 300,000/yr target. Peak was 243,770 in 2019-20.

completionsByTenure.title: New Build Completions by Tenure
completionsByTenure.summary: Completions split by private enterprise (80%+), housing associations, and local authorities (~3,000/yr).

newBuildSize.title: Average New Build Floor Area
newBuildSize.summary: Average internal floor area of new build homes. Declined from 83 sqm in 2003 to 76 sqm in 2023.

sizeIntl.title: International New Build Size Comparison
sizeIntl.summary: Average new build floor area by country. UK (76 sqm) among the smallest. Australia 229, US 201, Germany 109.

epcNewBuilds.title: New Build EPC Lodgements
epcNewBuilds.summary: Quarterly EPC lodgements for new domestic properties in England & Wales. Peaked at ~255k/yr in 2019, dropped during COVID, around 210k in 2025. A near-real-time proxy for housing completions.

brickDeliveries.title: Brick Deliveries (Seasonally Adjusted)
brickDeliveries.summary: Monthly seasonally adjusted brick deliveries in Great Britain (millions). Tracks material demand from builders. Around 168m/month in early 2018, declined to ~118m by late 2025.

datasets.title: Immigration & Demographics
datasets.summary: Net migration, visa breakdown, asylum applications, population projections, age structure, and dependency ratios.

datasets.title: Industrial Strategy
datasets.summary: Industrial output by sector, manufacturing trends, and structural economic composition.

datasets.title: Infrastructure
datasets.summary: Broadband (FTTP, gigabit coverage), rail (punctuality, journeys), and road (traffic, condition) metrics.

datasets.title: Investment
datasets.summary: UK gross fixed capital formation trend, asset breakdown, and international comparison.

international.title: International Comparison
international.summary: UK investment as % of GDP compared to OECD peers. UK near bottom of G7.

datasets.title: Justice & Crime
datasets.summary: Crime trends, police workforce, prison population, court backlog, and charge rates.

datasets.title: Local Authority Spending by Council Area
datasets.summary: Per-authority net current expenditure for 218 upper-tier local authorities across all four UK nations (England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland), 2017-18 to 2024-25. Breakdown by service area: education, adult social care, children's social care, highways, housing, environment, cultural, planning, public health, police, fire, central services. England from MHCLG Revenue Outturn, Scotland from LGFS, Wales from StatsWales, NI from Audit Office.

authorities.title: Per-Authority Service Spending
authorities.summary: Net current expenditure by service for each upper-tier authority across the UK. Kent highest at £2.7bn total, typical authority ~£660m. Social care accounts for 30-60% of spending depending on area.

datasets.title: Local Government Finance
datasets.summary: Council spending by service, funding sources, council tax levels, financial health (section 114 notices, reserves, audit backlog), social care squeeze, core spending power, and workforce trends.

serviceSpending.title: Service Spending by Category
serviceSpending.summary: Net current expenditure by service area. Adult social care £25.2bn, children's social care £15.6bn in 2024-25. Total excluding education £89.8bn.

fundingSources.title: Revenue Funding Mix
fundingSources.summary: Share of council revenue by source. Council tax rose from 34% (2010-11) to peak 52% (2019-20), settling at 46% (2024-25) as government grants were cut then partially restored.

councilTax.title: Average Band D Council Tax
councilTax.summary: Average Band D council tax rose from £1,439 (2010-11) to £2,280 (2025-26), a 58% increase in cash terms.

councilTaxByRegion.title: Council Tax by Region
councilTaxByRegion.summary: Band D council tax varies by area: London £1,982, Metropolitan £2,289, Unitary £2,366, Shire £2,344 (2025-26).

section114.title: Section 114 Notices
section114.summary: Seven councils issued section 114 notices between 2018 and 2023: Northamptonshire, Croydon, Slough, Thurrock, Woking, Birmingham, Nottingham.

reserves.title: Usable Reserves
reserves.summary: Local authority usable reserves rose from £13bn (2010-11) to £31.2bn peak (2021-22), then fell to £27bn (2024-25).

auditBacklog.title: Audit Backlog
auditBacklog.summary: Outstanding unaudited council accounts peaked at 918 (2022-23), reduced to ~100 after 2024 statutory backstop.

workforce.title: Local Government Workforce
workforce.summary: Council FTE fell from 1,510k (2010) to 970k (2025), a 36% reduction. Excludes police, fire, centrally-funded teachers.

socialCareShare.title: Social Care Share of Spending
socialCareShare.summary: Social care rose from 42% to 47% of non-education council spending between 2010-11 and 2024-25.

coreSpendingPower.title: Core Spending Power per Person
coreSpendingPower.summary: Real-terms core spending power per person fell 27% from 2010-11 to 2015-16. In 2024-25 it remains 18% below 2010-11 levels.

datasets.title: Mayoral & Combined Authority Spending
datasets.summary: Per-authority net current expenditure and revenue sources for 12 combined/mayoral authorities and the Greater London Authority, 2017-18 to 2024-25. Total spending grew from £5.7bn to £10.8bn. GLA accounts for 72% including Metropolitan Police (£4.4bn) and TfL (£1.8bn). Funding breakdown: government grants, council tax/mayoral precept, levies on constituent councils, earned income.

authorities.title: Per-Authority Service Spending and Revenue
authorities.summary: Net current expenditure by service plus funding sources (government grants, council tax, levies, earned income) for each combined authority. GLA largest at £7.8bn, GMCA second at £862m.

datasets.title: Mental Health
datasets.summary: NHS talking therapies referrals and recovery rates, children and young people mental health service access, Mental Health Act detentions, suicide rates, and psychiatric morbidity prevalence.

datasets.title: Money Supply & Inflation
datasets.summary: M4 broad money supply, Bank of England quantitative easing (APF holdings and cash flows), CPI inflation, real wage growth, purchasing power erosion, international central bank balance sheet comparison, nominal vs real government debt, mortgage approvals, cash ISA deposits, consumer credit, credit card lending, and effective mortgage interest rates.

m4ToGdp.title: M4 Money Supply and GDP
m4ToGdp.summary: M4 grew from £867bn (2000) to £3.2tn (2025). M4/GDP ratio rose from 86% to 116%, peaking at 139% in 2020.

apfHoldings.title: APF Gilt Holdings
apfHoldings.summary: Bank of England gilt purchases peaked at £895bn in January 2022. Reduced to £488bn by end-2025 through quantitative tightening.

apfCashFlows.title: APF Cash Flows to Treasury
apfCashFlows.summary: APF transferred £124bn to Treasury between 2012-22, then reversed. OBR estimates lifetime net cost at £134bn.

cpiRate.title: CPI Annual Inflation Rate
cpiRate.summary: CPI averaged close to 2% target from 2000-2020, spiked to 9.1% in 2022, returned to 3.2% in 2025.

realWages.title: Real Wage Growth
realWages.summary: Real wages fell in 6 of 7 years from 2008-14, and again in 2022 (-3.3%). Positive real growth resumed in 2024.

purchasingPower.title: Purchasing Power Index
purchasingPower.summary: £1 in 2000 buys approximately 55p of goods in 2025 terms, a 45% erosion in purchasing power.

debtNominalVsReal.title: Government Debt: Nominal vs Real
debtNominalVsReal.summary: PSND rose from £311bn (2000-01) to £2,805bn (2024-25) in nominal terms. In real terms (2000-01 prices), the increase is smaller.

balanceSheets.title: Central Bank Balance Sheets
balanceSheets.summary: BoE balance sheet peaked at 44% GDP (2021), BoJ at 137% (2022), ECB at 60% (2022), Fed at 35% (2022). All now contracting.

mortgageApprovals.title: Mortgage Approvals for House Purchase
mortgageApprovals.summary: Monthly mortgage approvals (SA). Peaked at ~133k/month (2003), crashed to ~26k during GFC, ~9k during COVID, currently ~62k. A leading indicator of housing market activity.

cashIsaDeposits.title: Cash ISA Deposits Outstanding
cashIsaDeposits.summary: Household cash ISA balances rose from £9.5bn (2000) to £458bn (Feb 2026). Accelerated from 2022 as interest rates rose.

consumerCredit.title: Consumer Credit Outstanding
consumerCredit.summary: Total unsecured lending to individuals (credit cards, personal loans, car finance, overdrafts). Rose from £84bn (2000) to £137bn (Feb 2026).

creditCardLending.title: Credit Card Lending Outstanding
creditCardLending.summary: Outstanding credit card debt held by UK individuals. Grew from £32bn (2000) to £80bn (Feb 2026).

mortgageRate.title: Effective Interest Rate on New Mortgage Lending
mortgageRate.summary: Average rate paid by new mortgage borrowers, weighted by loan value. Fell from 7% (2000) to below 2% (2021), rose to 5% (2023), now around 4%.

datasets.title: NHS Funding
datasets.summary: NHS budget in real terms, spending per capita, international health expenditure comparison (OECD), spending by category (staff, capital, other), and capital spending trends including backlog maintenance.

datasets.title: NHS Waiting Lists
datasets.summary: NHS referral-to-treatment waiting times, A&E performance, waiting lists by specialty and procedure.

datasets.title: NHS Workforce
datasets.summary: NHS HCHS staff headcount and FTE by staff group, vacancy rates by profession, and sickness absence rates.

staffByGroup.title: Staff by Group (FTE)
staffByGroup.summary: NHS HCHS workforce grew from 1.05m FTE in 2010 to 1.38m in 2025, up 31%.

vacancies.title: Vacancy Rates
vacancies.summary: Overall vacancy rate peaked at 9.7% in Q3 2022, fell to 6.7% by Q3 2025.

sickness.title: Sickness Absence
sickness.summary: Sickness rate was 4.1-4.4% pre-pandemic, rose to 5.8% in 2021, at 5.2% in 2025.

datasets.title: UK Population
datasets.summary: Mid-year population estimates for the UK and constituent countries from 1971 to 2025. UK population reached 69.3 million in mid-2024 (England 58.6M, Scotland 5.5M, Wales 3.2M, Northern Ireland 1.9M). Provisional mid-2025 estimate is 69.5 million.

annual.title: UK Population (Mid-Year Estimates)
annual.summary: Annual mid-year estimates from 1971. UK population grew from 55.9M (1971) to 69.3M (2024). England accounts for 85% of the total. Growth has accelerated since 2000, driven primarily by net migration.

datasets.title: Productivity
datasets.summary: Output per hour worked (index and level), sector breakdown, and international comparison.

international.title: International Productivity Comparison
international.summary: UK output per hour relative to G7 peers. UK trails US, Germany, and France.

datasets.title: Research & Development
datasets.summary: R&D spending by sector (government, business, higher education), as % of GDP, and international comparison.

bySector.title: R&D Expenditure by Sector
bySector.summary: UK GERD by funding source: business (£30.5bn), government (£2.7bn), higher education (£11.2bn) in 2022.

gdpPct.title: R&D as % of GDP
gdpPct.summary: UK R&D spending at 2.36% of GDP in 2022, below OECD average of 2.73%.

datasets.title: Personal Safety & Crime
datasets.summary: Violent crime, knife and firearms offences, homicides, sexual offences, domestic abuse, fraud, anti-social behaviour, crime outcomes, reoffending rates, fear of crime, and crime rates by police force area.

violentCrime.title: Violence Against the Person
violentCrime.summary: Police recorded violent crime. 2,060k offences in 2023-24, up from 846k in 2002-03.

homicide.title: Homicides
homicide.summary: 590 homicides in 2023-24, of which 229 involved a knife or sharp instrument.

sexualOffences.title: Police Recorded Sexual Offences
sexualOffences.summary: 193k in 2023-24. Sharp rise from 2014-15 driven by recording practice changes.

crimeOutcomes.title: Charge/Summons Rate
crimeOutcomes.summary: Overall charge rate fell from 16.3% in 2014-15 to 5.5% in 2023-24.

cityRates.title: Crime Rate by Police Force Area
cityRates.summary: Cleveland highest (131 per 1,000), Surrey lowest (55). National average 92.

datasets.title: Government Spending
datasets.summary: Total managed expenditure, spending as % of GDP, tax receipts, departmental spending trends, and debt.

datasets.title: Business Startups & Entrepreneurship
datasets.summary: Business births and deaths, survival rates, sector breakdown, high-growth firms, and equity investment.

survival.title: Survival Rates by Cohort
survival.summary: 1 to 5 year survival rates for business birth cohorts. 5-year survival around 38%.

highGrowth.title: High-Growth Firms
highGrowth.summary: Firms with 20%+ annualised employment growth over 3 years.

datasets.title: Taxation
datasets.summary: Tax receipts by type, tax burden as % of GDP, tax gap, income tax concentration by percentile, corporation tax rate history, personal allowance thresholds, and international comparisons.

receiptsByType.title: Tax Receipts by Type
receiptsByType.summary: HMRC collected £858.6bn in 2024-25. Income tax (£310bn), NICs (£174bn), and VAT (£171bn) are the three largest sources.

taxBurden.title: Tax Burden as % of GDP
taxBurden.summary: UK tax burden at 35.3% of GDP in 2024-25, forecast to reach 38.0% by 2029-30, a post-war high.

intlTaxBurden.title: Tax-to-GDP International Comparison
intlTaxBurden.summary: UK at 35.3% compared to OECD average of 33.7%. Ranges from 17.7% (Mexico) to 43.8% (France).

taxGap.title: Tax Gap
taxGap.summary: Estimated at £46.8bn (5.3%) in 2023-24, up from 4.8% in 2021-22. Corporation tax accounts for 40% of the gap.

taxConcentration.title: Income Tax Concentration
taxConcentration.summary: Top 1% paid 29.1% of all income tax in 2022-23, up from 21.3% in 1999-2000. Top 10% paid 59.8%.

corpTaxRate.title: Corporation Tax Main Rate
corpTaxRate.summary: Fell from 52% in 1982 to 19% in 2017, raised back to 25% in April 2023.

thresholds.title: Personal Allowance & Thresholds
thresholds.summary: Personal allowance frozen at £12,570 since 2021-22, extended to April 2031.

taxpayersByBand.title: Income Taxpayers by Rate Band
taxpayersByBand.summary: 34.5m income taxpayers in 2024-25. Higher and additional rate taxpayers rising due to threshold freeze.

datasets.title: Transport Fares
datasets.summary: Rail and bus & coach fare price indices from ONS CPIH and RPI. Since 2015, bus fares rose 89% (index 188.7) vs rail fares up 45% (index 145.2) vs general inflation of 40% (CPIH 140.0). RPI series goes back to 1987 showing bus fares rose 571% and rail fares 450% since Jan 1987.

cpihMonthly.title: Transport Fare Indices (CPIH, 2015=100)
cpihMonthly.summary: Monthly CPIH indices from Jan 2015. Bus fares index reached 188.7 by Feb 2026, rail fares 145.2, overall CPIH 140.0. Bus fares have risen nearly twice as fast as general inflation.

rpiMonthly.title: Transport Fare Indices (RPI, 1987=100)
rpiMonthly.summary: Monthly RPI indices from Jan 1987. Bus fares reached index 670.7 by Feb 2026, rail fares 550.3. Both far outpaced general RPI inflation over this period.

datasets.title: Unemployment & Labour Market
datasets.summary: UK labour market rates from 1992 to present. Unemployment rate 5.2%, employment rate 75.0%, economic inactivity rate 20.8% (latest quarter). Long-term sickness is the fastest-growing reason for inactivity, rising from around 600k in 1993 to over 1 million. 1.88 million people are unemployed.

quarterly.title: Labour Market Rates (quarterly)
quarterly.summary: Unemployment rate (16+), employment rate (16-64), and economic inactivity rate (16-64), seasonally adjusted from 1992. Unemployment peaked at 10.7% in 1993 and 8.5% in 2011. Current rate is 5.2%.

inactivityReasons.title: Economic Inactivity by Reason
inactivityReasons.summary: Reasons for inactivity among 16-64 year olds from 1993: long-term sick (2.8m, largest category), students (2.4m), looking after family/home (1.3m, declining), retired (1.0m), and other (592k). Long-term sickness overtook students as the largest reason and has risen sharply since 2019.

datasets.title: Water Industry
datasets.summary: Water company performance, leakage rates, pollution incidents, and storm overflow events.

datasets.title: Welfare Spending
datasets.summary: DWP benefit expenditure by category (children, working age, pensioners) and by individual benefit type (State Pension, housing, disability, incapacity, unemployment, Pension Credit, Carer's Allowance). Covers 1999/00 to 2024/25 outturn plus OBR forecast to 2030/31. Total spending reached £289bn in 2024/25, with pensioner benefits accounting for 57%.

byCategory.title: Welfare Spending by Category
byCategory.summary: Total benefit expenditure split by children, working age, and pensioners. Pensioner benefits grew from £67bn in 1999/00 to £165bn in 2024/25. Includes OBR forecast years to 2030/31.

byBenefit.title: Spending by Benefit Type
byBenefit.summary: Annual expenditure on individual benefits. State Pension is largest at £124bn, followed by housing benefits and disability payments. Covers 1999/00 to 2024/25.

datasets.title: Public Sector Workforce
datasets.summary: Workforce size and trends in NHS, education, police, and university sectors.

datasets.title: Youth Unemployment
datasets.summary: Youth (16-24) unemployment rate and economic inactivity rate from 1992 to present. Unemployment rate was 16.1% in 2025 Q4, with 38.8% economically inactive (mostly in education). Youth unemployment peaked at 22.5% in 2011 during the post-financial crisis recovery.

quarterly.title: Youth Labour Market (16-24)
quarterly.summary: Youth unemployment rate peaked at 22.5% in 2011-Q4, fell to 10.5% in 2019-Q4, spiked to 15.3% during COVID, and reached 16.1% in 2025-Q4. Economic inactivity for 16-24s is around 38.8%, mostly due to education participation.
